# Swiss Longevity Trends, Mortality Compression, and Actuarial Implications for Life Insurance and Pension Systems, 1988-2024

## Abstract

This report examines historical longevity trends in Switzerland from 1988 to 2024 using period life tables from the Human Mortality Database (HMD). The analysis is organized around total, female, and male populations and deliberately separates the main life-table indicators: the survivorship function \(l_x\), the death distribution \(d_x\), the mortality probability \(q_x\), curtate life expectancy at birth \(e_0\), curtate life expectancy at age 65 \(e_{65}\), total expected lifetime conditional on survival to age 65 \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point, interpreted as the modal age at death among adult-old ages. The results suggest sustained mortality improvement, a persistent but narrowing gender gap, a rightward movement in the age distribution of deaths, and visual evidence consistent with mortality compression and survival-curve rectangularization. The 2020 observation is treated as a temporary period-life-table disruption compatible with the COVID-19 period, followed by apparent recovery in 2024. The report then connects the demographic findings to healthy life expectancy, Swiss pension policy, life-insurance market structure, and the Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APVB) for a whole life insurance contract. The main actuarial conclusion is product-specific: mortality improvement tends to increase annuity and pension liabilities by extending payment duration, while it can reduce the present value of a fixed whole life death benefit under positive discounting by delaying the expected claim.

## 1. Introduction

Mortality assumptions are central to actuarial science because life-contingent liabilities depend on both the probability of death and the timing of death. In a life table, a reduction in age-specific mortality probabilities \(q_x\) changes the number of survivors \(l_x\), shifts the deaths \(d_x\) across ages, and affects present values through the timing of future benefits. For death benefits, the insurer is exposed to the probability and timing of claims. For annuities and pensions, the main exposure is systematic longevity risk: the possibility that an entire insured or pensioned population lives longer than expected when premiums, reserves, or contribution rates were determined. Standard actuarial texts express these problems through survival probabilities, life-table functions, curtate future lifetime, and actuarial present values (Spedicato, [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]; Valdez, 2019).

Switzerland is an appropriate high-longevity case for this report. It combines high observed survival, a mature multi-pillar retirement system, and a substantial life insurance and occupational pension sector. The first pillar old-age and survivors' insurance is commonly referred to as AHV/AVS, while the mandatory occupational pension system is known as BVG/LPP. In such a setting, sustained survival improvement is not only a demographic observation. It affects retirement duration, pension financing, insurer reserves, solvency assessment, and the pricing of products whose cash flows depend on death or survival.

The report is retrospective rather than predictive. It uses HMD period life tables for Switzerland over ten benchmark years: 1988, 1992, 1996, 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020, and 2024. The empirical work examines total, female, and male populations separately. The aim is not to estimate a full stochastic mortality model, but to show how Swiss longevity has changed historically through a sequence of actuarial indicators and charts. For this reason, the report deliberately separates life expectancy, old-age expectancy, Lexis Point, survivorship, deaths, mortality probabilities, and APVB pricing rather than treating them as one broad longevity summary.

The report proceeds in a degrouped structure. Section 2 describes the data and actuarial methodology. Section 3 defines the main life-table indicators. Sections 4, 5, and 6 analyze female, male, and total population longevity separately. Sections 7, 8, and 9 then examine \(l_x\), \(d_x\), and \(q_x\) as separate empirical objects. Section 10 synthesizes Lexis Point, mortality compression, and rectangularization. Section 11 discusses Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham fitting. Sections 12 to 14 consider healthy life expectancy, Swiss pension policy, and the life insurance market. Section 15 applies the findings to whole life insurance APVB. Sections 16 and 17 provide discussion and conclusion.

## 2. Data and Actuarial Methodology

### 2.1 Data Source and Sample Design

The empirical analysis uses Swiss period life tables from the Human Mortality Database (HMD). The tables are treated as a 1x1 life-table structure, meaning one-year age groups by one-year calendar periods (Human Mortality Database, 2026). The selected benchmark years are 1988, 1992, 1996, 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020, and 2024. This design provides a regular historical grid over approximately 36 years while keeping the number of tables manageable for figure-based actuarial interpretation.

The population is separated into total, female, and male life tables. This separation is necessary because sex-specific mortality differences remain material for life insurance pricing, pension valuation, and demographic interpretation. The total population provides a national aggregate mortality profile. The female and male series reveal whether the aggregate pattern is driven by a common movement or by sex-specific differences in the level and speed of mortality improvement.

The report uses selected years rather than every annual HMD table. This means that the charts are designed to show structural changes over time, not every short-run fluctuation. The inclusion of 2020 is analytically important because it captures a pandemic-period mortality environment, while 2024 provides a later benchmark for comparison. The interpretation remains cautious because life-table charts alone cannot identify causes of death or separate temporary shocks from persistent changes without additional decomposition.

### 2.2 Period Life Table Interpretation

A period life table summarizes mortality conditions observed in a particular year and applies those conditions to a hypothetical cohort. It is not a cohort life table and does not describe the actual mortality experience of a real birth cohort as it ages through future calendar years. This distinction is important for Switzerland because observed mortality improvement from 1988 to 2024 implies that a real cohort born in one year would not necessarily face the same age-specific mortality rates at all future ages.

The interpretation of \(e_0\), \(e_{65}\), \(l_x\), \(d_x\), and \(q_x\) is therefore conditional on the period framework. A rise in period \(e_0\) means that the period life table for that year implies longer expected lifetime under that year's mortality schedule. It does not mean that every individual born in that year will experience exactly that lifetime. Similarly, a 2020 period disruption can affect the period table strongly even if its long-term cohort implications differ.

### 2.3 Population Groups and Benchmark Years

The ten benchmark years are chosen to preserve the original report's empirical design. The sequence captures long-run improvement from the late 1980s, pre- and post-2000 changes, the pre-pandemic 2016 point, the 2020 shock year, and the 2024 recovery point. The four-year spacing is not a formal forecasting model. It is a historical sampling design that supports visual comparison of life-table shapes and summary indicators.

The total population series is used for national interpretation. The female series is used because Switzerland's female population begins from a high longevity baseline and remains the higher-survival group throughout the report. The male series is used because the original calculations show stronger improvement from a lower starting point. This separation supports a balanced interpretation: Switzerland's longevity improvement is a national phenomenon, but the timing and magnitude of improvement differ by sex.

### 2.4 Calculation Approach and Limitations

The author calculations use HMD period life tables to compute or plot the life-table variables. The complete numerical values for all 30 combinations of year and sex are described in the original draft as being stored in a detached spreadsheet titled `Life_Expectancy_30Tables_Complete_CHE.csv`; this file is not present in the current folder and should be checked before final submission if exact table values are required. For this report, the values explicitly stated in the old draft are preserved where they are available, while missing exact total-population values are marked as requiring verification.

The main limitations are methodological. First, period tables do not incorporate future mortality improvement after the observation year. Second, selected benchmark years may miss annual volatility between the chosen points. Third, visual figure interpretation is not the same as formal statistical testing. Fourth, the report uses chart-based evidence for mortality compression, rectangularization, and Gompertz-Makeham fit, so conclusions about these concepts are framed as "suggests" or "is consistent with" rather than as formal statistical results.

## 3. Life Table Indicators and Conceptual Separation

### 3.1 Survivorship Function \(l_x\)

The survivorship function \(l_x\) gives the number of lives surviving to exact age \(x\) out of an initial radix \(l_0\), often 100,000 lives. When normalized as \(l_x/l_0\), it can be interpreted as the period life-table probability of surviving from birth to age \(x\). In this report, \(l_x\) is used to study the shape of survival over age and to identify rectangularization. A curve that remains close to its initial level through young and middle ages before dropping at older ages suggests that a large share of the hypothetical period cohort survives to old age.

### 3.2 Death Distribution \(d_x\)

The death distribution \(d_x\) measures the number of deaths between exact ages \(x\) and \(x+1\):

$$
d_x = l_x - l_{x+1}.
$$

This indicator is not the same as \(q_x\). It describes where deaths occur in the life table, not the conditional probability of death for someone already alive at age \(x\). In the Swiss charts, \(d_x\) is used to assess whether deaths are spread across many ages or concentrated around advanced ages. A rightward shift in the main \(d_x\) peak and a taller or narrower peak are visual signs consistent with mortality compression.

### 3.3 Mortality Probability \(q_x\)

The one-year mortality probability \(q_x\) is the conditional probability of death between ages \(x\) and \(x+1\), given survival to age \(x\):

$$
q_x = \frac{d_x}{l_x}.
$$

The \(q_x\) curve gives the risk-rate view of the life table. It is typically high at age zero, low during childhood, low but sometimes uneven during young adulthood, and steeply increasing at older ages. In this report, \(q_x\) explains the mechanism behind changes in \(l_x\) and \(d_x\): if \(q_x\) declines at young, middle, or old ages, more lives remain in the life table and deaths are shifted later.

### 3.4 Curtate Life Expectancy \(e_x\)

For a life aged \(x\), curtate life expectancy is the expected number of complete future years lived. In life-table notation, the report uses:

$$
e_x = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{l_{x+k}}{l_x}.
$$

The two main expectancy indicators are life expectancy at birth \(e_0\) and life expectancy at age 65 \(e_{65}\). The report also uses \(e_{65}+65\), the total expected lifetime conditional on survival to age 65. This transformed indicator is useful because it places old-age survival on the same age scale as \(e_0\) and the Lexis Point. It should not be confused with \(e_{65}\) itself: \(e_{65}\) is remaining curtate future lifetime after age 65, while \(e_{65}+65\) is the implied total expected age at death conditional on reaching 65.

### 3.5 Lexis Point

The Lexis Point is used as the modal age at death among adult-old ages. It is the age at which \(d_x\) reaches its main old-age maximum. The Lexis Point is not life expectancy, and it is not a fixed biological maximum lifespan. It is a descriptive marker for the most common age at death within the period life table. Pitacco and Carmeci (2019) use this type of modal-age indicator, together with life expectancy at birth and total expected lifetime at age 65, to describe longevity trends and mortality concentration.

### 3.6 Analytical Framework

The analytical framework connects six empirical indicators. Mortality improvement is observed through lower or later-rising \(q_x\). Higher survival to older ages appears in \(l_x\). The location and concentration of deaths are observed in \(d_x\). Average lifetime from birth is summarized by \(e_0\). Old-age survival is summarized by \(e_{65}\) and \(e_{65}+65\). The most common adult-old age at death is summarized by the Lexis Point.

These indicators support three actuarial concepts. The first is mortality improvement, meaning a reduction in death probabilities over time. The second is rectangularization of the survival curve, where survival remains high until later ages and then declines more steeply. The third is mortality compression, where deaths are increasingly concentrated in a narrower age interval at older ages. These concepts matter because the same mortality change affects products differently. It increases expected payment duration for pensions and annuities, but it can reduce the APVB of a fixed death benefit when mortality improvement delays the expected claim under a positive discount rate.

## 4. Female Longevity Analysis

### 4.1 Female Summary Indicators

The female population begins from a high longevity baseline. In the author calculations, female curtate life expectancy at birth \(e_0\) rises from 80.23 years in 1988 to 84.57 years in 2020 and 85.40 years in 2024. The old-age indicator \(e_{65}+65\) rises from 83.97 years in 1988 to 86.68 years in 2020 and 87.46 years in 2024. The Lexis Point moves from 86 years in 1988 to 91 years by 2008 and remains at 91 years in 2024.

![Female longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image1]

The figure plots three different indicators and should not be read as a single life-expectancy curve. \(e_0\) summarizes average lifetime from birth under each period table. \(e_{65}+65\) isolates the expected total age at death for those who have already survived to age 65. The Lexis Point identifies the modal age at death in the adult-old portion of the death distribution. Their simultaneous upward movement suggests that female longevity improvement is visible both in general population survival and in old-age survival.

### 4.2 Interpretation of Female \(e_0\) and \(e_{65}+65\)

The rise in female \(e_0\) indicates improvement across the life course. Because \(e_0\) is sensitive to early-life and adult mortality, part of the increase may reflect the reduced weight of younger-age deaths in the period life table. The rise in \(e_{65}+65\) is more directly connected to retirement-age survival. It indicates that female individuals who reach age 65 are expected to live to older total ages under later-period mortality conditions.

This distinction matters actuarially. For death-benefit products issued at younger ages, lower mortality at adult ages can delay the expected timing of claims. For annuities and pensions, the rise in \(e_{65}+65\) is more directly relevant because it increases the expected duration of benefit payments after retirement. Therefore, the female trend cannot be summarized only as "higher life expectancy"; it affects different cash-flow structures through different mechanisms.

### 4.3 Female Lexis Point and Mortality Concentration

The female Lexis Point rising from 86 to 91 years, then remaining near 91, indicates that the most common adult-old age at death shifts toward an advanced age. This is evidence of concentration of deaths around old ages, but it should not be treated as a fixed biological boundary. The chart suggests that deaths are less spread across earlier ages and more concentrated near the old-age mode.

The original draft interpreted this pattern as the near elimination of premature mortality. A more defensible interpretation is narrower: the female period life tables show substantially reduced younger-age mortality relative to old-age mortality, so the age distribution of deaths becomes more concentrated at later ages. Premature deaths are not eliminated; they become less visible relative to the old-age death peak in the selected life-table profiles.

### 4.4 Female Actuarial Implications

For pension and annuity valuation, the female series implies longer expected payment duration. A pension system that pays benefits from retirement age is directly exposed to the rise in \(e_{65}+65\) and to the concentration of deaths around advanced ages. For whole life insurance, the same higher survival profile tends to defer the expected death benefit. Under positive discounting, this contributes to a lower APVB for females than for males, as later shown in the pricing section.

The female analysis also provides a benchmark for gender comparison. Females remain the higher-longevity population in the available values, but their rate of improvement is less rapid than the male catch-up observed from the lower male baseline. This means the female section is not only an isolated sex-specific analysis; it is the reference point against which male convergence is measured.

## 5. Male Longevity Analysis

### 5.1 Male Summary Indicators

The male population shows the strongest improvement over the observation period. In the author calculations, male \(e_0\) increases from 73.40 years in 1988 to 80.50 years in 2020 and 81.92 years in 2024, an increase of 8.52 years between 1988 and 2024. Over the same period, \(e_{65}+65\) rises from 79.80 years to 84.94 years. The male Lexis Point advances from 83 years in 1988 to 90 years in 2024.

![Male longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image8]

The figure indicates that male longevity improvement is not confined to one indicator. \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point all move upward. The increase in \(e_0\) reflects broad life-course mortality improvement, while the increase in \(e_{65}+65\) shows that old-age survival also improves. The Lexis Point movement indicates that the most common adult-old age at death shifts toward older ages.

### 5.2 Male Catch-Up and Gender Gap Reduction

The male increase in \(e_0\) is larger than the female increase reported in the old draft. Female \(e_0\) rises by about 5.17 years from 1988 to 2024, while male \(e_0\) rises by about 8.52 years. The female advantage therefore remains, but the gap narrows. Using the reported 2024 values, female \(e_0\) is 85.40 and male \(e_0\) is 81.92, leaving a difference of about 3.48 years.

This catch-up effect is actuarially relevant. Male mortality remains higher at many ages, but the relative difference between male and female mortality schedules changes over time. Pricing, reserving, and experience monitoring should therefore not assume that a historical gender gap remains constant. The appropriate conclusion is not that male and female mortality have become identical, but that the male deficit has narrowed materially in the selected HMD period tables.

### 5.3 Male Survival Curve Evidence

The male survival function provides shape-based evidence behind the summary indicators. The earlier male life table shows a more visible decline before old age, while later years show a flatter survival profile through working ages and early old age. This pattern is consistent with a reduction in premature and middle-age mortality.

![Male survival function, Switzerland, selected benchmark years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image9]

The chart should be interpreted as visual evidence rather than causal proof. It suggests that a larger share of the hypothetical male period cohort survives to older ages in later years. It does not identify why mortality improved. Claims about workplace regulation, health behavior, mental health, accident prevention, or medical access would require separate evidence. The actuarial conclusion is sufficient without those causal claims: improved male survival changes expected claim timing and retirement survival.

### 5.4 Male Death Distribution and Mortality Probability Evidence

The old draft emphasizes that male mortality compression is visible in the distribution of deaths. The male death distribution in later years appears more concentrated around an advanced modal age than in the 1988 profile. This supports the interpretation that male lifetimes became less dispersed in the period life-table representation.

![Male death-distribution supporting panel, Switzerland, selected benchmark years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image10]

![Additional male death-distribution panel, Switzerland, selected benchmark years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image11]

The male \(q_x\) evidence is also relevant. The old report discusses a reduction in the young-adult "accident hump". The cautious version is that the later male \(q_x\) profiles appear smoother and lower in young and middle adulthood than the 1988 profile. This is compatible with a reduction in young-adult excess mortality, but the report does not verify cause-of-death composition. The result should therefore be framed as an observed life-table pattern rather than a proven social mechanism.

![Male mortality probability curve, Switzerland, selected benchmark years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image13]

![Additional male mortality-probability panel highlighting young-adult ages. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image15]

### 5.5 Male Actuarial Implications

Male improvement has two product-specific effects. For pensions and annuities, higher male survival raises expected payment duration and reduces the historical offset created by earlier male deaths. For whole life insurance, improved male survival delays the expected payment of a fixed death benefit, lowering APVB under positive interest. Nevertheless, because male mortality remains higher than female mortality at many ages, the male APVB remains above the female APVB in the pricing chart.

## 6. Total Population Analysis

### 6.1 National Aggregate Indicators

The total population series provides the national baseline for the empirical analysis. It combines the historically higher female survival profile with the faster male improvement observed over the period. Exact total-population benchmark values should be checked against the underlying HMD calculation table before final submission [NEED EMPIRICAL VALUE], but the chart indicates a persistent upward movement in aggregate Swiss longevity between 1988 and 2024.

![Total population longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image16]

The figure plots \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point for the total Swiss population. The upward movement of \(e_0\) suggests broad mortality improvement across the population. The upward movement of \(e_{65}+65\) shows that improvement is also visible after age 65. The Lexis Point remaining around advanced ages indicates that the most common old-age death age is located near the 90-91 age region in the later benchmark years.

### 6.2 Total Population as Policy Baseline

The total population is especially relevant for public pension interpretation because AHV/AVS is not priced separately as a private insurance portfolio by sex-specific underwriting class in the same way as some life insurance products. A national aggregate profile is therefore useful for discussing retirement duration and the expected number of beneficiaries at advanced ages.

However, the total population should not replace sex-specific analysis. It is an average of different mortality schedules. If male mortality improves faster while female longevity remains higher, the total series can hide important shifts in composition and convergence. The report therefore uses the total series for national interpretation but keeps the female and male sections separate for actuarial specificity.

### 6.3 COVID-19 Period Disruption

The 2020 benchmark appears as a disruption in several figures. This is compatible with the timing of the COVID-19 pandemic, but the charts alone do not identify causes of death. The apparent recovery by 2024 suggests that the 2020 observation did not overturn the longer-run pattern of Swiss mortality improvement. A more precise pandemic analysis would require annual age-specific death rates, cause-of-death decomposition, and comparison with 2019, 2021, 2022, and 2023 rather than only the selected benchmark years.

### 6.4 Gender Comparison and Convergence

The gender comparison has two main results. First, females maintain higher life expectancy throughout the observation period. Second, male improvement is faster, so the gender gap narrows. This means the male series closes part of the historical survival deficit, although it does not fully converge with the female series.

The Lexis Point also converges at advanced ages. In 1988, the modal age at death is reported as 86 for females and 83 for males. By 2024, the corresponding values are 91 and 90. This does not mean that both sexes have the same mortality schedule. It means that the ages at which deaths are most concentrated have moved closer together. For actuarial work, this supports the need to examine both average life expectancy and the shape of the age-at-death distribution.

![Comparison of male and female death distributions, Switzerland. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image12]

## 7. Survival Function Analysis: \(l_x\)

### 7.1 What the Survival Function Shows

The survival function \(l_x\), especially when normalized as \(l_x/l_0\), shows the proportion of the life-table cohort surviving to each age. It is the clearest chart for analyzing rectangularization because it displays whether survival remains high through young, adult, and early-old ages before declining rapidly.

![Survival function \(l_x/l_0\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image2]

The Swiss survival curves show a higher and flatter profile in later benchmark years. In the original report's visual interpretation, the curves remain close to the maximum survival level through much of youth and adulthood, then drop more steeply at older ages. The later curves also shift to the right, meaning that a larger share of the period life-table cohort survives to older ages before the steep decline begins.

### 7.2 Demographic Meaning

Demographically, this pattern is consistent with reduced premature and middle-age mortality. A survival curve that declines gradually across many ages indicates that deaths are dispersed across the life course. A more rectangular curve indicates that the majority of deaths occur after a larger share of the population has reached older ages.

The interpretation should remain conditional. Rectangularization does not mean everyone survives to a fixed old age, and it does not mean young or middle-age deaths are absent. It means that, relative to earlier period tables, these deaths have less influence on the overall survival profile.

### 7.3 Actuarial Meaning

For pensions and annuities, a higher \(l_x\) at older ages means more expected survivors receiving benefits. This directly affects expected payment duration, funding pressure, and longevity risk. For whole life insurance, higher survival shifts the expected death benefit to later durations, which increases discounting and can lower APVB for a fixed benefit. The survival curve therefore provides the first bridge between demographic change and product-specific actuarial consequences.

## 8. Death Distribution Analysis: \(d_x\)

### 8.1 What the Death Distribution Shows

The death distribution \(d_x\) shows the number of life-table deaths occurring between ages \(x\) and \(x+1\). It transforms the survival curve into an age-at-death distribution. This is the most direct chart for studying mortality compression because it shows whether deaths are spread over many ages or concentrated near a particular old-age mode.

![Death distribution \(d_x\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image3]

The Swiss \(d_x\) chart suggests a rightward movement of the main death peak and a stronger concentration of deaths around advanced ages in later years. In the original draft, this was described as people dying at nearly the same age. A more precise academic interpretation is that the period life-table distribution of deaths becomes more concentrated around a later modal age.

### 8.2 Early-Life and Old-Age Panels

The early-life panels help explain why \(e_0\) improves. The magnified early-life death distribution suggests a substantial decline in infant and young-age deaths across the period. This reduces the influence of early deaths on average lifetime from birth.

![Early-life and older-age death distribution panels. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image4]

![Magnified early-life death distribution. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image5]

The old-age panels help explain mortality compression. When fewer deaths occur early and more people reach old age, the death distribution becomes more concentrated around the old-age modal region. This does not mean mortality risk disappears. It means the age at which the risk is realized shifts and becomes more concentrated.

### 8.3 Actuarial Meaning

The \(d_x\) chart is useful for product valuation because it identifies when benefits are expected to be paid or received. For a whole life death benefit, the distribution of death ages determines the probability-weighted timing of the claim. For an annuity or pension, the same shift implies that payments continue for longer before death terminates the payment stream. The death distribution therefore explains why the same mortality trend can reduce APVB for death benefits while increasing annuity and pension liabilities.

## 9. Mortality Probability Analysis: \(q_x\)

### 9.1 What the Mortality Probability Curve Shows

The mortality probability \(q_x\) gives the conditional probability of dying within one year after reaching age \(x\). It is the risk-rate view of mortality and is distinct from both \(l_x\) and \(d_x\). The Swiss chart shows the usual human mortality shape: an initial infant component, low childhood and young-adult mortality, and a steep old-age increase.

![Mortality probability \(q_x\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image6]

Across the benchmark years, the later \(q_x\) curves appear lower or later-rising over many age intervals. The most important actuarial interpretation is that lower \(q_x\) feeds directly into higher \(l_x\), later deaths in \(d_x\), and higher \(e_0\) and \(e_{65}+65\). The \(q_x\) curve is therefore the mechanism behind the other charts rather than just another descriptive figure.

### 9.2 Old-Age Mortality Improvement

Old-age \(q_x\) is central for pensions and annuities. If \(q_x\) declines after age 65, more people survive to receive later benefit payments. This increases the expected duration of retirement payments and strengthens systematic longevity risk. The rise in \(e_{65}+65\) is consistent with this interpretation.

The curves also show that mortality continues to rise sharply at advanced ages. Compression does not remove mortality risk; it relocates risk toward older ages. In practical actuarial work, this means reserve sensitivity shifts toward old-age mortality assumptions and mortality-improvement rates.

### 9.3 Young-Adult Mortality and the Male Accident-Hump Issue

The original report emphasizes that male mortality improvement appears particularly visible in young and middle adulthood. The cautious interpretation is that the later male \(q_x\) profiles show a weaker young-adult elevation than earlier profiles. This may be described as a reduction in an accident-hump-like pattern if the chart visually supports it.

The report does not prove the causes of that change. It should not attribute the pattern to workplace regulation, mental health, behavioral changes, or safety policy unless separate evidence is added. The academically defensible conclusion is that the male \(q_x\) curve became lower and smoother in the selected young-adult age range, contributing to male catch-up and to the rectangularization of the male survival curve.

## 10. Lexis Point, Mortality Compression, and Rectangularization

### 10.1 Lexis Point as Modal Age at Death

The Lexis Point summarizes the age at which the death distribution reaches its main adult-old maximum. It is useful because it captures the movement of the death peak in one number. In the report's calculations, the female Lexis Point moves from 86 to 91, the male Lexis Point moves from 83 to 90, and the total population Lexis Point is concentrated around the advanced-age region in the later benchmark years.

This is not the same as life expectancy. \(e_0\) is an average; \(e_{65}+65\) is a conditional expected total age; the Lexis Point is a mode. The three indicators can move together, but they answer different questions. The Lexis Point is especially useful for analyzing the timing and concentration of deaths, whereas life expectancy summarizes an expected value.

### 10.2 Mortality Compression

Mortality compression means that deaths are increasingly concentrated within a narrower age range at older ages. In the Swiss charts, the evidence comes mainly from \(d_x\): the old-age death peak shifts rightward and appears more concentrated in later benchmark years. The rise in the Lexis Point is a numerical summary of the same movement.

Compression should not be overstated. It does not mean that every individual dies at the same age, and it does not mean uncertainty disappears. It means the period life-table distribution of deaths becomes less dispersed across earlier ages and more concentrated around advanced ages. For actuarial work, this changes the timing of expected cash flows and the distribution of risk over age.

### 10.3 Rectangularization of the Survival Curve

Rectangularization is observed in \(l_x/l_0\). A more rectangular survival curve remains close to one for longer and then falls more steeply at older ages. In Switzerland, the later benchmark-year curves show this pattern more strongly than earlier curves. This supports the interpretation that premature and middle-age mortality have become less prominent in the period life-table profiles.

Rectangularization and mortality compression are related but not identical. Rectangularization describes the shape of the survival curve. Compression describes the concentration of deaths. The first is observed primarily through \(l_x\); the second through \(d_x\) and the Lexis Point. The report keeps them separate because each one has a different actuarial interpretation.

### 10.4 Synthesis Across Indicators

The strongest interpretation comes from the joint evidence. Lower \(q_x\) at many ages raises \(l_x\). Higher \(l_x\) at older ages delays deaths. The delayed deaths appear in \(d_x\) as a rightward shift and concentration of the death peak. The Lexis Point summarizes that shift. \(e_0\) and \(e_{65}+65\) quantify average lifetime and retirement-age survival. Together, these indicators support the conclusion that Swiss mortality improvement from 1988 to 2024 is consistent with both rectangularization and mortality compression.

Actuarially, the same pattern has different implications by product. For pensions and annuities, more survivors at older ages increase payment duration. For whole life insurance, later death delays the death benefit and can reduce the APVB under positive interest. This product-specific contrast is central to the report.

## 11. Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham Mortality Fitting

### 11.1 Gompertz Model

The Gompertz model is a classical model of adult and old-age mortality in which the force of mortality increases exponentially with age. In simplified form, it can be written as:

$$
\mu_x = Bc^x, \quad B>0,\; c>1.
$$

On a logarithmic scale, the Gompertz pattern appears approximately linear over adult and old ages. This makes it useful for summarizing senescent mortality. In the Swiss 2024 fitting chart, the old-age empirical points appear broadly consistent with an exponential increase in mortality over the fitted range.

### 11.2 Gompertz-Makeham Model

The Gompertz-Makeham model adds an age-independent component:

$$
\mu_x = A + Bc^x.
$$

The Makeham term \(A\) is often interpreted as background mortality that is not strongly age-dependent, such as accidental or environmental mortality. In the old report, the Gompertz-Makeham comparison is used to discuss whether the 2024 Swiss mortality profile is dominated by senescent mortality. This is a useful actuarial framing, but it should be treated cautiously.

![Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham fit to 2024 mortality probabilities. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image7]

![Male Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham diagnostic panel, 2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image14]

### 11.3 Cautious Interpretation of the 2024 Fit

The visual fit suggests that old-age Swiss mortality in 2024 is broadly compatible with a Gompertz-type increase. It may also suggest that age-dependent old-age mortality dominates over the fitted older-age interval. However, the chart alone cannot prove that background mortality has disappeared, that the Makeham parameter is statistically insignificant, or that Switzerland has reached a pure senescent mortality structure.

A formal conclusion would require parameter estimates, standard errors, residual diagnostics, goodness-of-fit statistics, the fitted age range, and sensitivity checks. Without these details, the Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham figures should be used as exploratory actuarial tools. They support the broader life-table interpretation, but they do not replace the direct evidence from \(l_x\), \(d_x\), \(q_x\), \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point.

## 12. Healthy Life Expectancy versus Total Life Expectancy

### 12.1 Conceptual Distinction

Total Life Expectancy (TLE), Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE), and Health-Adjusted Life Expectancy (HALE) describe related but distinct dimensions of longevity. TLE measures expected years lived. HLE and HALE adjust expected years for time lived in less than full health. The HMD analysis in this report uses curtate period life-table measures, while WHO health-expectancy values use a different source framework. The two should therefore be compared conceptually, not mechanically merged.

The distinction matters because mortality improvement and morbidity improvement are not identical. A population can live longer without gaining the same number of years in full health. Conversely, healthy years may increase if morbidity is delayed. Pitacco and Carmeci (2019) discuss the importance of distinguishing the length of life from the quality or health state of those years. For actuarial work, this distinction affects pensions, health insurance, long-term care, and household retirement planning.

### 12.2 WHO TLE and HALE Evidence for Switzerland

The WHO country data for Switzerland provide a basis for comparing TLE and HLE/HALE. The values used in the old draft state that TLE at birth increased from 79.7 years in 2000 to 83.3 years in 2021, while HLE/HALE increased from 68.4 years to 71.1 years over the same period. On these values, the implied difference between TLE and HLE/HALE increases from 11.3 to 12.2 years. These values should be checked against the WHO data table before final submission [CHECK WHO FIGURE], but the WHO country page is the appropriate source category for this comparison (World Health Organization, n.d.).

| ![WHO life expectancy chart for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image17] | ![WHO sex-specific life expectancy chart for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image18] |
| :-- | :-- |

The sex-specific WHO chart is consistent with the HMD finding that female total life expectancy remains higher than male total life expectancy. However, WHO TLE and HMD curtate \(e_0\) are not identical measures. The report therefore uses WHO data to contextualize health-state issues rather than to replace the HMD life-table calculations.

### 12.3 The TLE-HLE Gap and Morbidity Interpretation

The TLE-HLE gap indicates the difference between expected years lived and expected years lived in full health. If the gap widens, the pattern is consistent with concern that additional years of life may include additional years with morbidity or functional limitation. This does not prove a full theory of morbidity expansion. It only suggests that longevity improvement should not be interpreted as a pure increase in healthy retirement years.

![WHO healthy life expectancy comparison for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image19]

The actuarial implication is that a longer lifetime can create multiple forms of exposure. Pension and annuity providers face longer payment duration. Health insurers and long-term care providers may face higher late-life care exposure if added years are lived with chronic illness or dependency. Public budgets may also be affected because pension, healthcare, and care needs are not independent in old age.

### 12.4 Limitations of the HLE/TLE Comparison

The WHO values in this report end in 2021, while the HMD life-table analysis extends to 2024. The HLE/TLE comparison therefore cannot be described as a 2024 health-expectancy analysis unless an updated WHO or national source is added. HALE also depends on morbidity weights and international estimation methods. For these reasons, the report uses the WHO evidence as a contextual health-state dimension rather than as a direct pricing input for the APVB calculation.

## 13. Swiss Pension and AHV/AVS Policy Implications

### 13.1 Swiss Multi-Pillar Pension Setting

Switzerland's pension system is directly exposed to the longevity trends documented in the HMD tables. The first pillar, AHV/AVS, is the public old-age and survivors' insurance system. The second pillar, BVG/LPP, is the occupational pension system and is designed to complement the first pillar through employment-related retirement provision (Swiss Confederation, n.d.-a). The third pillar is private pension provision and voluntary retirement saving (Swiss Confederation, n.d.-b).

This structure matters because mortality improvement affects each pillar differently. In a pay-as-you-go first pillar, longer retirement duration affects the relationship between contributors and beneficiaries. In occupational pensions, longer survival affects conversion rates, reserves, longevity assumptions, and asset-liability management. In private saving, longer life increases the horizon over which households must finance consumption and care.

### 13.2 Pay-As-You-Go Pressure and Retirement Duration

The HMD analysis shows that survival after age 65 improves over time. This is visible in the upward movement of \(e_{65}+65\) and in the survival curve remaining high into older ages. For a pension system, this means that the expected period over which retirement benefits are paid becomes longer unless retirement age, contributions, benefits, or financing rules adjust.

This should not be framed as a mechanical proof that a particular policy is necessary. A life table does not determine policy by itself. It provides the demographic mechanism that makes pension sustainability sensitive to old-age survival. The policy response depends on social preferences, contribution design, benefit adequacy, labour-market conditions, and fiscal choices.

### 13.3 AHV 21 Reform

The AHV 21 reform is relevant because it adjusts retirement parameters in a setting where old-age survival has increased. The Federal Social Insurance Office states that AHV 21 entered into force on January 1, 2024. The reform introduced the term "reference age" and sets a common reference age of 65 for men and women, while raising the women's reference age gradually from 64 to 65. The staged schedule is 64 years and 3 months in 2025, 64 years and 6 months in 2026, 64 years and 9 months in 2027, and 65 from 2028 (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2025).

The demographic link is that women have historically shown higher life expectancy in the HMD results, while the legal reference age is moving toward alignment between men and women. The reform should be described as a policy response occurring in the context of longevity improvement, not as a policy directly proven by the charts.

### 13.4 Thirteenth AHV Pension Payment

The approved 13th AHV pension payment adds a benefit-design dimension. The Federal Social Insurance Office states that the 13th AHV pension will first be paid in December 2026 and that financing was still under parliamentary discussion on the referenced page. It also states that the payment is made once per year in December as a supplement to the December pension for recipients of an old-age pension (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2026).

Longevity affects the fiscal relevance of such a measure because recurring benefits apply over the expected lifetime of retirees. If more people survive to older ages and retirement duration lengthens, recurring pension enhancements apply to more person-years of benefit payment. This does not mean the 13th AHV pension is caused by longevity alone; it means longevity is part of the actuarial environment in which benefit design and financing must be assessed.

### 13.5 Pension Implications of HLE/TLE

The HLE/TLE section adds an important caution. Longer total life expectancy does not necessarily mean equivalent increases in healthy working life. If a material TLE-HLE gap persists, retirement-age policy and pension sustainability should be discussed alongside health, disability, and long-term care considerations. An actuarial pension analysis based only on total survival may miss late-life health-state risk.

## 14. Swiss Life Insurance Market Context

### 14.1 Gross Written Premiums and Life Segment

The Swiss life insurance sector provides the institutional setting for the report's actuarial interpretation. FINMA's 2024 insurance market report states that total Swiss insurance Gross Written Premiums (GWP) reached CHF 150.0 billion in 2024, an increase of 6.7% from 2023. Within that market, the life insurance sector generated CHF 23.666 billion in GWP, a decrease of 8.4% from the previous year. FINMA notes that the decline was influenced by reinsurance accepted and by a special effect connected with the conversion of a foreign branch into a foreign subsidiary. Life-insurer annual profits increased by 21.7% to CHF 1.587 billion (FINMA, 2025).

GWP is a flow measure. It indicates annual business volume, not the full size of accumulated obligations. The old draft sometimes treated premium volume and reserve scale as if they were the same concept. In this report they are separated: GWP measures premiums written during the year; technical liabilities and actuarial reserves measure obligations accumulated on the balance sheet.

### 14.2 Technical Liabilities, Investments, and SST

The balance-sheet dimension is more directly connected to longevity risk than GWP alone. FINMA reports that Swiss life insurers held a balance-sheet total of CHF 290.236 billion, investments of CHF 267.558 billion, and technical liabilities of CHF 252.269 billion in 2024. FINMA also reports an SST solvency ratio of 227% for life insurers in 2024 (FINMA, 2025).

The Swiss Solvency Test (SST) ratio indicates the relationship between available and required risk-bearing capital under the Swiss solvency framework. A ratio above 100% indicates that the sector meets the solvency requirement, while a higher ratio suggests a larger regulatory capital buffer. It should not be interpreted as eliminating longevity risk. Long-duration liabilities remain sensitive to mortality assumptions, interest rates, guarantees, and product mix.

### 14.3 Group Life and Occupational Pension Relevance

The composition of life premiums confirms the connection between life insurance and occupational pensions. FINMA reports that group life occupational pension schemes accounted for CHF 14.375 billion of life GWP in 2024, or 60.7% of life-insurance GWP. FINMA also reports gross actuarial reserves of CHF 216.372 billion for life insurers, with group life occupational pension schemes representing 57.8% of those reserves (FINMA, 2025).

This composition is important because it links life insurers to BVG/LPP-related business. Mortality improvement affects the valuation of pension guarantees, conversion assumptions, annuity-like obligations, and group life portfolios. The market context therefore reinforces the report's main actuarial point: longevity risk is not only a public-pension issue but also a private-insurance and occupational-pension issue.

### 14.4 Market Concentration and Major Participants

FINMA's direct Swiss business table indicates that the six largest life insurers accounted for 85.5% of direct Swiss life premium volume in 2024. Swiss Life had the largest share at 41.0%, followed by Helvetia Leben at 12.4%, Baloise Leben at 10.2%, AXA Leben at 9.0%, Allianz Suisse Leben at 7.2%, and Zurich Leben at 5.7% (FINMA, 2025).

These figures support describing the market as highly concentrated, with a small group of major market participants accounting for most direct life premium volume. They do not by themselves prove market profitability, market power, or systemic importance for each firm. Those claims would require additional evidence. The relevant actuarial conclusion is that mortality and longevity assumptions can affect large portfolios managed by a limited number of insurers.

### 14.5 Relationship to Longevity and Mortality Risk

Life insurers write products exposed to both mortality risk and longevity risk. Death benefits depend on the probability and timing of death. Annuities, pension guarantees, and retirement-income products depend on survival. The same mortality improvement can therefore move liabilities in different directions. In a market with substantial technical liabilities and a large group-life occupational pension segment, updated mortality tables and dynamic experience monitoring are central to pricing, reserving, and solvency management.

## 15. Whole Life Insurance Pricing and APVB

### 15.1 Product Definition and Assumptions

This section evaluates a simplified whole life insurance contract issued to a policyholder aged 35. The contract pays a fixed death benefit of EUR 400,000 at the end of the year of death. The technical interest rate is fixed at 2.5% per annum. The valuation is repeated for the ten benchmark years from 1988 to 2024 and calculated separately for male, female, and total Swiss period life tables.

The valuation is deterministic and life-table based. It excludes expenses, profit loading, lapses, surrender values, underwriting classes, taxes, stochastic interest rates, capital costs, and currency conversion. The benefit is denominated in EUR while the mortality basis is Swiss. The results should therefore be read as a mortality-driven APVB comparison rather than as market premium quotations.

### 15.2 APVB Formula

Let \(x=35\) be the entry age, \(B\) be the sum assured, \(i=0.025\) be the technical interest rate, and \(v=(1+i)^{-1}\) be the annual discount factor. Let \({}_kp_x\) be the probability that a life aged \(x\) survives \(k\) years, and let \(q_{x+k}\) be the probability of death during the following year. For a benefit paid at the end of the year of death, the Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APVB) is:

$$
APVB = B \times \sum_{k=0}^{\omega-x-1} v^{k+1} \cdot {}_kp_x \cdot q_{x+k}.
$$

The term \({}_kp_x q_{x+k}\) is the probability that the insured survives to the start of policy year \(k+1\) and then dies during that year. The factor \(v^{k+1}\) discounts the benefit from the end of the year of death back to issue. This is the standard life-contingent present-value logic used for whole life insurance (Spedicato, [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]).

### 15.3 Empirical APVB Trend

![APVB of a EUR 400,000 whole life insurance contract issued at age 35, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image20]

The APVB chart shows a downward trend from 1988 to 2024 for all three population series. This pattern is consistent with the mortality improvement documented earlier. When survival improves, the expected death benefit is paid later. Because the technical interest rate is positive, a later expected payment receives more discounting and has a lower present value at issue.

The chart also shows the expected ordering by sex. Male APVB is higher than female APVB because male mortality probabilities are generally higher at the relevant ages, so the expected death benefit is paid earlier. Female APVB is lower because higher survival probabilities defer the expected payment. The total population lies between the male and female series because it combines both mortality profiles.

### 15.4 COVID-19 Disruption and 2024 Recovery

The 2020 observation appears to interrupt the downward APVB trend. This is compatible with a pandemic-period mortality shock: if mortality rises temporarily, the probability of earlier death increases, the expected payment date moves forward, and the present value of the fixed death benefit rises. The 2024 point appears to return closer to the longer-run downward pattern. This should be interpreted as a period-table recovery signal rather than as proof that all pandemic-related effects have disappeared.

The old draft reports that the APVB decreases by more than 15% over the full period. The direction is supported by the chart and by the actuarial mechanism, but the exact percentage should be verified against the underlying spreadsheet titled `APVB_WholeLife_Switzerland_Results` before final submission: [CHECK APVB RESULT TABLE].

### 15.5 Contrast with Annuities and Pensions

The APVB result highlights the central product-specific contrast of the report. Mortality improvement lowers the APVB of a fixed whole life death benefit when the discount rate is positive, because the expected claim is delayed. The same mortality improvement increases annuity and pension liabilities because payments continue for a longer expected period. This contrast explains why mortality improvement cannot be labelled simply as favourable or unfavourable for insurers. Its financial effect depends on the benefit trigger and payment pattern.

Dynamic mortality tables are therefore necessary. A static mortality basis from an earlier high-mortality year may overstate the present value of a fixed death benefit in later years. For pensions and annuities, outdated mortality assumptions may understate survival duration and reserves. The 2020 disruption also shows that actuaries must distinguish short-run mortality shocks from long-run improvement trends.

## 16. Discussion

The degrouped analysis shows that Swiss longevity improvement is visible through several distinct channels. \(e_0\) rises, indicating broad life-course mortality improvement. \(e_{65}+65\) rises, indicating longer expected survival after retirement age. The Lexis Point shifts toward older ages, indicating a later modal age at death. The survival function becomes more rectangular, the death distribution becomes more concentrated around advanced ages, and \(q_x\) declines or shifts downward over many age ranges.

The gender analysis adds important structure. Females remain the higher-longevity population, but male improvement is faster from a lower baseline. The male catch-up is visible in \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), the Lexis Point, and the male survival profile. This supports continued sex-specific mortality analysis while also showing that the historical gap is not fixed. The total population series then provides the national aggregate pattern relevant for public pension interpretation.

The report also separates mortality compression from morbidity questions. Mortality compression describes the timing and concentration of deaths. It does not automatically imply compression of morbidity. The WHO HLE/TLE comparison suggests that Switzerland's high total life expectancy should be interpreted alongside a material gap between total years lived and expected healthy years. This matters for health insurance, long-term care, pension adequacy, and retirement-age policy.

The institutional sections show why the demographic results matter. AHV/AVS and BVG/LPP are exposed to longer survival after retirement. FINMA data show that the Swiss life sector has substantial technical liabilities and a large occupational-pension-related group life segment. The APVB section then demonstrates that the same mortality improvement can have opposite effects across products: it increases survival-contingent liabilities but can reduce the present value of a fixed death-contingent benefit.

The analysis is therefore not only a descriptive longevity report. It is an actuarial interpretation of how the timing of death changes the timing of cash flows. The core insight is that actuaries must model \(l_x\), \(d_x\), \(q_x\), \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point separately before drawing pricing or policy conclusions.

## 17. Conclusion

This report reconstructs a historical actuarial analysis of Swiss longevity trends from 1988 to 2024 using HMD period life tables. The evidence suggests sustained mortality improvement, a persistent but narrowing gender gap, a rightward shift in the modal age at death, and chart evidence consistent with mortality compression and survival-curve rectangularization. The female population remains the higher-longevity group, while the male population shows stronger catch-up from a lower baseline.

The actuarial implications are product-specific. For pensions and annuities, longer survival increases expected payment duration and strengthens systematic longevity risk. For the simplified whole life insurance contract, mortality improvement tends to lower APVB because the fixed death benefit is expected to be paid later and is more heavily discounted. The 2020 observation illustrates the importance of distinguishing temporary period shocks from long-run mortality improvement.

The report has several limitations. It uses period rather than cohort life tables, selected benchmark years rather than the full annual HMD series, and visual chart interpretation rather than formal statistical testing. The Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham section does not report fitted parameters or residual diagnostics. The APVB calculation excludes expenses, lapses, underwriting, taxes, currency effects, capital costs, and stochastic interest rates. Exact total-population values and the reported APVB percentage decline should be verified against the author's spreadsheets [NEED EMPIRICAL VALUE; CHECK APVB RESULT TABLE]. WHO health-expectancy figures should also be checked against the current WHO data table before final submission [CHECK WHO FIGURE].

Future research could extend the analysis using cohort life tables, annual HMD series, stochastic mortality models such as Lee-Carter or Cairns-Blake-Dowd, formal measures of mortality compression, annuity pricing, and long-term care insurance valuation. A further extension could also decompose Swiss mortality improvement by age and cause of death, especially around the 2020 pandemic-period disruption.

## References

Federal Social Insurance Office. (2025). *AHV 21*. Swiss Confederation. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/ahv-21

Federal Social Insurance Office. (2026). *Umsetzung der Initiative fur eine 13. AHV-Rente*. Swiss Confederation. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/umsetzung-13-ahv-rente

FINMA. (2025). *Insurance market report 2024*. Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority. https://www.finma.ch/en/~/media/finma/dokumente/dokumentencenter/myfinma/finma-publikationen/versicherungsbericht/20250903-versicherungsmarktbericht-2024.pdf

Human Mortality Database. (2026). *Switzerland period life tables*. University of California, Berkeley, and Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.mortality.org/

Pitacco, E., & Carmeci, G. (2019). *Longevity trends: Historical aspects and recent issues*. DemoLab Paper 01/2019. MIB Trieste School of Management.

Spedicato, G. ([CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]). *Life contingencies*. In *Computational Actuarial Science with R*.

Swiss Confederation. (n.d.-a). *The 2nd pillar: Occupational pension*. ch.ch. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-2nd-pillar/

Swiss Confederation. (n.d.-b). *The 3rd pillar: Private pension provision*. ch.ch. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-3rd-pillar/

Valdez, E. A. (2019). *Life tables and selection: Lecture weeks 4-5*. Math 3630. [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]

World Health Organization. (n.d.). *Switzerland: Health data overview for the Swiss Confederation*. WHO Data. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://data.who.int/countries/756
